back: A cache miss occurs when the CPU requests data that is not found in the cache memory. The CPU must then access the slower main memory to retrieve the data, incurring a significant time penalty.

front: What is Write Allocation policy on a cache miss?
back: Write Allocation (also called Fetch on Write) is a policy where, on a write miss, the block is fetched from main memory into the cache before the write operation is performed.
No-Write Allocate is a policy where on a write miss, the data is written directly to main memory without loading the block into the cache.

front: What happens during a cache line fill?
back: When a cache miss occurs and the block needs to be allocated, a cache line fill transfers the block from main memory to an available cache line, updating the tag and valid bits.

front: What is the difference between write-through and write-back in relation to allocation?
back: Write-through always writes to both cache and main memory. Write-back writes only to cache initially and delays writing to main memory until eviction. Both can use write allocation.

front: What is the difference between write-miss and read-miss allocation?
back: On a read miss, the block is always loaded into the cache. On a write miss, the allocation policy determines whether to load the block (write allocate) or not (no-write allocate).
Write allocate is beneficial when there is spatial locality - if we write to one location, we likely will read/write nearby locations soon, making cache storage valuable.
No-write allocate is preferred when there is temporal or spatial locality but the data is unlikely to be accessed again soon, or when the memory footprint is large.

front: What is the role of the eviction policy when allocating on a cache miss?
back: If the cache is full on a miss, an eviction policy (like LRU, FIFO, or Random) must select a victim line to replace before the new block can be allocated.

front: What is the relationship between write allocate and fetch on write?
back: Write Allocate and Fetch on Write are synonymous terms describing the same policy where a block is fetched into cache on a write miss.

front: How does write-through with no-write-allocate work?
back: On a write miss, the data is written directly to main memory without loading the block into the cache. Subsequent writes also go directly to memory.

front: How does write-back with write-allocate work?
back: On a write miss, the block is loaded into cache, the write modifies the cache line, and the dirty bit is set. The data is written to main memory only when the line is evicted.

front: What is the performance impact of write allocation?
back: Write allocation introduces additional latency on a write miss because the block must be fetched from memory. However, it can improve performance if subsequent accesses benefit from the cached data.

front: What determines which allocation policy to use?
back: The allocation policy depends on the memory access pattern, the presence of spatial/temporal locality, the write-to-read ratio, and the desired performance vs complexity tradeoff.
